US Macro Forecast

Vintage 2026-07  ·  Data to 2026Q1  ·  Updated 2026-07-13 20:38 UTC  Backtest report ↗
ForecastsNowcastThe long-run viewAnchor diagnosticsWhat drives the forecastWhat drove the economyHow the economy responds to shocksSummary tablesWhat makes up the modelMethodologyThe forecasts, explainedThe model

A regularly updated forecast for the US economy from a 28-variable Bayesian VAR: a statistical model that learns the joint dynamics of the main US macroeconomic series from their history and projects them forward as a full distribution around each path. It is estimated on quarterly public data back to 1986 and re-run each vintage. The sections below run from the headline forecasts through a current-quarter nowcast and the five-year view to the model’s out-of-sample track record.

Nowcast — 2026Q2

The model's current-quarter real GDP read, and how the monthly data released so far have moved it. Beta.
1.8%q/q annualised · MF-BVAR nowcast
Current-quarter (2026Q2) real GDP · Model baseline 2.0%
The mixed-frequency BVAR nowcast folds the partial monthly data already released for the current quarter into the model via entropic tilting (Krüger-Clark-Ravazzolo 2017); it is the primary current-quarter read. It is a side output — the headline forecast is unchanged.
Annualised GDP growth by estimate
EstimateMedian (68% band)
Model baseline+1.96%-0.34 to +4.37%
MF-BVAR nowcast+1.81%+0.10 to +3.54%
MF nowcast: 6 pooled chains, split-R̂ 1.026. Fold-in: ESS 1.00, mean-only (nowcast/forecast variance ratio 0.34).
How the partial monthly data move the forecast
The Model baseline re-run with the monthly indicators already released for the current quarter (the nowcast, red), against the path that ignores them (grey). The marked points on each chart are the current forecast quarter, both values labelled.
What moved the GDP nowcast
Each released indicator is compared with what the Model baseline expected for it; the gap (the “news”) propagates through the model's dynamics to the current-quarter (2026Q2) GDP nowcast. The bar and the last table column are that propagated contribution, in percentage points of q/q annualised GDP growth, and they sum to the net news. The nowcast draws on monthly data across 2 partial quarters (2026Q2, 2026Q3); where a series printed in more than one, the table shows its largest surprise and its total contribution.

Net news-0.15
Released and Model-expected are each series' own basis — year-on-year % for activity and prices, level % for interest rates. Surprise = released − expected; a bold surprise is more than two posterior standard deviations from the model's prior.

The long-run view

Where each forecast object takes the economy over the next five years, and whether the long-run relationships hold together. Hover or tap a chip to flip that forecast's nested 68/90% bands. Coherence checks, not accuracy claims.
This module tracks where each published forecast takes the economy over the next five years — far enough out that the paths reflect each forecast's steady state rather than the news, and the horizon of the growth densities below — and whether the long-run relationships that should hold the system together actually do. Four forecasts appear, named by where their discipline comes from. Model baseline is the model's own forecast under the standard statistical prior, with no outside information — the benchmark for what anchoring adds. Theory-anchored builds long-run discipline into the model itself, through a prior that holds economically meaningful relationships (spreads, expenditure shares) together rather than adjusting the output afterwards. Benchmark-anchored reweights the model's paths toward public long-run benchmarks (CBO potential growth and published long-run rates). Analyst-anchored is conditioned on the analyst baseline near term and anchored to its long-run views. The theory-anchored forecast is a cross-check whose role is fixed in advance. Long-run accuracy cannot be scored in real time at these horizons, so the diagnostics here are coherence checks against theory and public benchmarks, not accuracy claims.
hover or tap to flip the bands · tap again to unpin · default: Model baseline
Densities are drawn over each forecast's 1–99% range with a heavy-tail-robust bandwidth; the far tails are reported as the shaded 90% bands above and the band-width column in the numbers table below.
Coherence checks
How to read this panel. Each chart shows an economically meaningful combination of variables — a spread or a great ratio — with its history (black) and each prior's forecast of it. Theory says the spreads and the output gap are stationary: a coherent long-run forecast keeps them fluctuating around a stable level, and a line that walks away is the model quietly pulling the system apart (this is exactly where an unanchored levels VAR fails first). The labour and consumption shares are different: in the post-1986 data they trend, so a forecast that keeps trending is the model being right, not drifting — the subtitle on each panel says which reading applies. The comparison shown isolates the new prior: the production prior (SOC) versus the long-run prior (PLR) on otherwise identical models. Watch the output-gap panel: the model baseline lets the gap drift away over the forecast; the theory-anchored forecast holds it near zero. That discipline — not any hard-coded number — is what the new object contributes.
hover or tap to flip the bands · tap again to unpin · default: Model baseline
The numbers
The numbers behind the pictures. GDP drift is the annualised median growth rate over forecast years 6–10, past the charted window: the model's implied steady-state growth, read against CBO potential (≈1.9%). The pure model baseline runs hot, the benchmark-anchored forecast is pulled to the target by reweighting, and the theory-anchored forecast lands near it from inside the model. Fed Funds median @5y is where each object settles the policy rate at the charts' horizon. Band width is the credible interval at year 5 — in-model discipline prices some extra far-horizon rate uncertainty, shown rather than hidden. Median max root measures system stability: levels models run deliberately close to 1; the long-run prior sits slightly higher, which is why its validation gate tracks drift and roots together.
Forecast objectGDP drift yrs 6–10 (CBO ≈ 1.9)Fed Funds median @5yFF 68% band width @5yGDP y/y 90% band width @5yMedian max root
Model baseline—4.6%7.8pp6.7pp—
Theory-anchored—5.1%7.1pp7.9pp—
Benchmark-anchored—3.5%7.6pp7.4pp—
Analyst-anchored—3.1%6.0pp5.7pp—
Model vs CBO long-run benchmarks
Each forecast object in levels against the Congressional Budget Office’s published potential GDP and natural rate of unemployment, over the same five-year window. The benchmark-anchored path is the one steered toward these; the others show where the model goes on its own.

Anchor diagnostics

The forecasts above are disciplined by external anchors, imposed by reweighting the model's posterior draws until each anchor is met. This table reports the health of that step for the current vintage: how many constraints were met exactly, and the minimum effective sample size (ESS) — the share of draws still carrying weight after reweighting. Low ESS means an anchor sits far from the model's own view; below 0.30 the anchor is refused rather than published on thin evidence.
Anchor set Applies toConstraints met Min ESSStatus

What drives the forecast — variance decomposition

Where the forecast's uncertainty comes from, under two ways of splitting it (tabs): by the variable group the surprise originates in, or by named economic shock. A third tab shows the same decomposition on a 32-variable research variant of the model whose extra variables buy a finer 10-shock menu — a candidate under evaluation, not the model behind this page's forecasts. This is a property of the estimated model's own dynamics — the Model baseline forecast; the anchors reweight paths afterwards and do not change it.

By variable group — recursive ordering

Share of each variable's forecast-error variance attributable to each shock group, by horizon. Shocks are grouped by the variable they originate in, under the model's recursive (Cholesky) ordering. Hover for shares.
How to read this. Each bar splits the uncertainty in a variable's forecast into the share coming from each family of shocks. A tall navy segment for GDP, for example, means most of the uncertainty about future GDP comes from real-activity shocks rather than from prices or financial conditions. Near term, a variable's own shocks dominate; further out, other blocks contribute more as the dynamics propagate. The split uses a recursive (Cholesky) ordering, so the exact shares depend on that ordering — but the broad ranking (which shocks matter most) is generally robust.

By named shock — sign restrictions

The same uncertainty split, but by named economic shock: nine shocks identified by sign and ranking restrictions (Demand, Investment, Financial, Monetary, Gov Spending, Supply, Oil Supply, Housing/Credit, Uncertainty), pooled here into blocks — including the combined Financial+Uncertainty family where both are named (the split within that family is softer than the family total, which is the robust object) — plus an exact Unidentified remainder. Mean shares across 250 accepted identifications. Hover for shares.
Why this is here. The variable-group tab asks “which variable’s surprises matter”; this tab asks “which economic story do they belong to” — the demand-type family, the supply-type family, or monetary policy. Read the grey honestly. With this identification method, naming 9 of the model’s 28 shocks implies a ∼68% expected “Unidentified” share before any data are consulted (a geometric property of set identification, not a defect); the measured grey for GDP at 5 years is 67%, to be read against that ∼68% baseline rather than against zero. The block sums shown here are the robust objects — they do not depend on how the method rotates shocks within a block. And the grey pool is not mysterious: a single statistically-extracted “main business-cycle” shock accounts for a median 91% of unemployment’s business-cycle variance on its own, and 58% even after removing everything the nine named shocks explain — the unnamed variance is concentrated, not diffuse, but naming its economic cause would require assumptions this dashboard does not impose (see docs/structural_tools.md).

Research model — 32 variables, 10 named shocks (on ice)

A research variant of the model, shown for the record next to the production scheme: the 28 production variables plus labor productivity, real hourly compensation, real federal receipts and house prices, re-estimated at full production settings. The extra variables buy a 10-shock menu that splits the generic supply shock into Technology / Labour Supply / Wage Bargaining and adds a Tax shock (765 accepted identifications). This model does not feed any forecast on this page — it is a candidate, on ice until it has been run through the full toolkit (conditioning, anchoring, validation, backtest).
The new shocks’ impulse responses — the supply split and Tax
One-standard-deviation shocks; % deviation for levels, percentage points for rates; shaded area is the 66% band. Hover for each shock's direction convention. As on the other IRF tabs, the model is in levels, so effects on levels are persistent rather than fading to zero — read shapes over the first two to three years rather than the drift at the tail.
What the research model adds. On this estimation the ten named shocks account for 36% of GDP’s 2-year forecast-error variance, with the supply-type family at 14% — of which Technology alone contributes 5.0% — and the new Tax shock at 2.9%. The split is bought by sign restrictions only: the real-wage response separates Technology from the two labour-market shocks, and the unemployment response separates Labour Supply from Wage Bargaining. Read the panels knowing what is imposed. The impact signs on GDP, prices, unemployment, wages, receipts and the funds rate are assumptions; the evidence is in the unrestricted responses — receipts rise persistently after a tax hike with no reversal, the Fed’s easing after a tax hike deepens over the first year, and productivity stays flat under wage-bargaining shocks. One caveat carried from review: the Labour Supply shock shows a small positive productivity response where theory expects roughly flat — some technology content bleeds through the wage-sign separator, so the Technology/Labour Supply boundary is softer than the supply-family total. A 12-shock extension (adding the Housing and Uncertainty columns of the production scheme) was measured and set aside: 4 acceptances per 5 million rotations — internally consistent but beyond this compute budget (docs/research/tier_design_2026-07.md).

What drove the economy — shock decomposition

The variance section above asks which shocks will drive forecast uncertainty; this asks which shocks did drive the data, back to the global financial crisis. Solid black line: the actual series. Dashed grey line: the model's no-shock baseline — where the series would have gone on the economy's own momentum from five years earlier, with every shock switched off. Bars: the shock families' contributions plus the non-structural Covid 2020 component (means across 150 accepted identifications), which sum exactly from the baseline to the actual value at every date. The GDP and unemployment y-axes are set from the non-pandemic quarters so the 2020–21 swings don't compress everything else; those bars and the actual line run off-scale there, with their exact values on hover. Same shock families and colours as the variance tab; the second tab shows the same decomposition under the 32-variable research variant's 10-shock menu. Hover for values.
Real GDP growth
CPI inflation
Unemployment rate
Federal funds rate
ActualNo-shock baseline (dashed)Demand-typeFinancial+UncertaintySupply-typeMonetary policyUnidentifiedCovid 2020
How to read one date. Take any quarter of the GDP panel: the gap between the dashed and solid lines is what shocks did to y/y growth over the preceding five years, and the bars name the families responsible — navy above the dashed line means demand-type shocks were lifting growth, green below means supply-type shocks were dragging it. GDP and CPI are shown as year-on-year rates (the four-quarter change, decomposed with both endpoints measured against the same five-year-earlier anchor, so the pieces add up exactly); the unemployment and funds rates are decomposed in levels. Four honest caveats. The baseline is re-anchored each quarter (always five years back), so read the bars vertically at a date rather than comparing bar-to-bar across long spans; shock recovery uses each posterior draw's end-of-sample volatility state. The near-black “Covid 2020” bar carries the transitory pandemic dislocation: the component of the 2020Q1–Q3 shocks that the model’s outlier layer (Carriero-Clark-Marcellino-Mertens 2024) classifies as one-off, plus its knock-on effect through the window anchor. The named bars carry only the persistent part of those quarters’ shocks, so they stay interpretable through the pandemic (the Bank of England’s convention for its own decomposition charts). The bar is exactly zero from 2025Q4, when the 20-quarter window clears 2020. The grey “Unidentified” pool is expected to be large (naming 9 of 28 shocks implies a ∼68% unnamed share by construction — see the variance tab); at the zero-lower-bound episodes it also absorbs genuine demand shocks, whose required interest-rate co-movement the ZLB suppresses. And the giant 2020 bars are real, not an artefact: they are the pandemic hitting inside the window.
Real GDP growth
CPI inflation
Unemployment rate
Federal funds rate
ActualNo-shock baseline (dashed)Demand-typeFinancialSupply-typeMonetary policyTaxUnidentifiedCovid 2020
The research model's version of the same object. These are the identical decompositions computed on the 32-variable research variant (on ice — see the variance section's research tab) under its 10-shock menu: the supply family here aggregates Oil Supply, Technology, Labour Supply and Wage Bargaining, and Tax appears as its own bronze family (means across 150 accepted identifications; contributions and the Covid-2020 component sum exactly from baseline to actual, as on the production tab). Reading rules, window and caveats are the production tab's. Two differences to hold in mind when comparing across tabs: the family definitions shift (this menu has no Housing/Credit or Uncertainty column, so the production tab's demand family and teal Financial+Uncertainty block are not the same objects as the blocks here), and the grey pool is not smaller despite the extra named shock — 10 named of 32 columns is a smaller named fraction than 9 of 28, and the finer supply/tax columns individually absorb little variance. The comparison that does hold: the dates each tab assigns to its demand, supply and monetary families broadly agree, which is the cross-model robustness the tab is here to show.

How the economy responds to shocks — impulse responses

Five structural views of the same model, one shown at a time (tabs): the named shocks of the production identification (the menu behind the variance and decomposition sections), a recursive (timing) identification of the financial shocks, sign restrictions for demand and monetary, external instruments for the monetary shock, and the max-share business-cycle shock (found by search, not assumption). That the methods agree where each is valid is a check on the structural machinery; each tab carries its own commentary and caveats.

Named shocks — the production scheme's full menu

All 9 named shocks of the production identification (the same accepted rotations behind the variance and decomposition sections), each a one-standard-deviation structural shock traced across the five headline responses over 12 quarters. Shaded area is the 66% band, pooling identification and parameter uncertainty. Hover for each shock's direction convention.
Why this is here. The other tabs show selected shocks under alternative identification methods; this one shows the production menu itself — the nine shocks whose variance shares and historical contributions the sections above report — so the three displays describe one identification. Each column is shown in its identifying direction (hover): most are expansionary normalisations, Monetary is a tightening, Oil Supply is an adverse supply disruption, and Uncertainty is displayed as a rise in uncertainty (it is identified through its calm mirror image; under linearity the flip is exact). Magnitudes are one-SD shocks rather than headline units, so compare shapes and relative sizes across shocks rather than reading absolute policy magnitudes; bands are wide by construction — sign restrictions set-identify, and the spread across accepted rotations is part of the honest uncertainty.

Recursive identification (CEGSQ) — financial shocks

Each financial shock (a row) traced across the five headline responses (columns), over 12 quarters. Each column has its own y-axis — % deviation for output/prices, percentage points for rates. Hover for values.
Shock sizes (illustrative episode magnitudes): Risk-off (credit spread +0.5pp) · Uncertainty (policy uncertainty +50%) · Equity sell-off (equities -10%). Because the model is linear, the responses scale in proportion to the shock.

Which shocks, and why these. These are the shocks this model identifies reliably — financial / risk shocks. Identification is by conditional forecasting (CEGSQ): the shocked financial variable is moved while the slow-moving real variables are held at their no-shock path for the first quarter (a recursive contemporaneous restriction), and the response is the difference from the no-shock forecast. Because financial variables are “fast” (they can react within the quarter), this ordering identifies them cleanly. Not shown: a monetary-policy shock (on the post-1986 sample the rate–activity comovement is demand-dominated, so a recursive funds-rate shock comes out wrong-signed) and an oil-supply shock (it requires sign restrictions plus an oil-production series this model does not carry). See the methodology note. One caveat shared by all the responses: the model is in levels, so a shock’s effect on a level (output, prices) is persistent rather than fading to zero.

Sign-restricted identification — demand & monetary

An aggregate demand shock and a monetary shock (rows), each traced across the same five responses (columns), identified by sign restrictions — a different method than the recursive tab — over 12 quarters. Demand is normalised to GDP +1% at impact, monetary to a +50bp funds-rate rise. Shaded area is the 68% band. Hover for values.
Why this is here. The recursive tab identifies shocks by a timing assumption. Sign restrictions are a different scheme: keep every model whose responses match an assumed sign pattern (a demand shock raises output and prices, etc.). That the two methods agree on the demand shock — output, prices and employment all move the textbook way — is a good check that the structural machinery works. The monetary shock is the interesting case: the recursive panel can’t identify it on this sample (the rate–activity comovement is demand-dominated, so a recursive funds-rate shock comes out wrong-signed), whereas sign restrictions — by imposing the uncontroversial contractionary pattern (a rate rise that cools output and prices) — recover the textbook transmission. The honest cost: it is set-identified, so the bands are wider and the pattern is partly assumed rather than discovered (only a minority of admissible models satisfy it — acceptance Demand 98%, Monetary 36%).

External instruments — Bauer-Swanson monetary surprises

A monetary shock identified from outside the model: minute-level interest-rate moves around every Fed announcement (Bauer-Swanson, 1988–2023), summed by quarter, pick out the part of the model’s surprises that is pure policy news. Rows: the raw surprise series and the information-purged variant (the Fed’s predictable response to news stripped out); both normalised to a +25bp funds-rate rise. Shaded area is the 68% band. Hover for values.
Why this is here. The sign-restrictions tab assumes the contractionary pattern; an external instrument lets the data supply it. The result is a cautionary tale worth seeing: the raw surprises are a strong instrument but come out wrong-signed (activity rises after a “tightening”) — at quarterly frequency they inherit the Fed’s tendency to surprise hawkish when the economy is strong, and that direction is statistically supported and robust (Raw surprises: robust F 21, GDP direction t +2.5). The info-purged series points the textbook way but is too faint at quarterly frequency to be conclusive (Info-purged: robust F 3, GDP direction t -0.8, not significant; shown to 12 quarters; its point pattern also depends on how the 2020 observations — which the data provider invalidates — are treated). Bands reflect model uncertainty only, not instrument-sampling uncertainty. Full diagnostics: the monetary-proxy experiment’s Anderson-Rubin tests.

The main business-cycle shock — max-share

The single shock that explains the most unemployment variance at business-cycle horizons (1½–8 years) — found by direct search over every possible shock, one per posterior draw, no sign assumptions. It accounts for a median 91% of that variance on its own. Traced across the same five responses over 12 quarters; sized at one standard deviation, signed so unemployment rises. Shaded area is the 68% band, spanning parameter and identification uncertainty jointly. Hover for values.
Why this is here. The variance tab shows that no small set of named shocks can claim much of the model's variance (an arithmetic property of that method, not a defect). This tab asks the reverse question: is the business cycle one big force or many small ones? The answer is emphatically one — a single statistically-extracted shock carries most of unemployment's cyclical variance, and its responses look like a recession in the quantities: activity falls, unemployment rises, the policy rate is cut. Prices, notably, barely move — itself a clue that this force is not a pure textbook demand shock. The honest caveat is the label. This method finds the shape of the dominant business-cycle force but cannot name its economic cause — “the thing that moves unemployment” may blend demand, financial and other impulses. Naming it would require assumptions (sign restrictions, instruments) of the kind the other tabs impose. The two approaches are complementary: named shocks with modest coverage, or dominant coverage without a name.

Methodology

This page publishes a regularly updated forecast for the United States from a 28-variable Bayesian vector autoregression (BVAR), estimated on quarterly Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) back to 1986 with four lags.

The model uses order-invariant stochastic volatility (Chan-Koop-Yu 2024) on top of a fixed Minnesota coefficient prior and a sum-of-coefficients prior. The stochastic-volatility specification, which is outlier-robust, lets the width of the uncertainty bands respond to the macroeconomic environment rather than being fixed. The posterior is sampled by Gibbs (10,000 draws, 5,000 burn-in, thinned to 1,000).

The page shows four forecasts that differ only in what is assumed, named by where each one's discipline comes from:

  • Model baseline (grey, headline). The model's own forecast with nothing imposed — the honest view of what the data and the estimated dynamics imply. It fits the short run well; because a levels VAR has no structure forcing convergence, its long horizon drifts (rates and inflation tend to climb), which is exactly what the anchored variants below are there to discipline.
  • Benchmark-anchored (navy). The model's own dynamics, with the long run gently steered toward published reference values — the Congressional Budget Office's potential growth and natural rate of unemployment, the FOMC's longer-run policy rate, and market-based inflation expectations — by entropic tilting at the five-year horizon. Nothing is imposed in the near term; the anchoring is a soft reweighting of the model's own paths, not a hard pin, so the bands stay smooth.
  • Theory-anchored (green). The same model with long-run economic relationships — spreads, expenditure shares, the output gap — built into the prior itself (a "priors for the long run" specification), so the long run is disciplined from inside the model rather than adjusted afterwards. No numeric target is imposed anywhere.
  • Analyst-anchored (red). Conditioned on a central analyst forecast for the near term — those values are imposed as hard quarterly pins through a Kalman filter and a Carter-Kohn smoother for roughly the first two years — and tilted to the analyst's annual averages in the long run.

Shaded areas are nested 68% and 90% credible bands, shown for one forecast at a time (hover or tap a chip above the charts to flip them); following central-bank fan-chart convention they fan out from the last observed data point.

The variance decomposition attributes each variable's forecast uncertainty to groups of structural shocks under a recursive ordering. This is a statistical model output, not advice; long-horizon interest-rate paths are the least reliable element and should be read as indicative.

The forecasts, explained

One model, four disciplines — what each published forecast assumes, at a glance.
Every forecast on this page comes from the same 28-variable Bayesian VAR estimated on the same data. They differ only in what disciplines the path — nothing (Model baseline), economic theory inside the prior (Theory-anchored), public long-run benchmarks applied afterwards (Benchmark-anchored), or a central analyst baseline (Analyst-anchored). The current-quarter nowcast additionally folds partial monthly data into the opening quarter. Read them together: where all four agree, the call is model-robust; where they fan apart, the gap tells you which assumption is doing the work.
Model baseline
How it gets there: each of the 28 equations is shrunk toward “next quarter looks like this quarter”, and the data decide everything else. Nothing in the prior says where any series should settle. Year-5 GDP growth: 2.7%.
Theory-anchored
How it gets there: 17 artificial pre-observations tell the estimator that the relationships between series (spreads, expenditure shares, the output gap) should not drift apart over long horizons. It does not pin any series to its sample average: every level and growth trend stays free; only the combinations are disciplined. Year-5 GDP growth: 1.9%.
Benchmark-anchored
How it gets there: the baseline’s own simulated paths are reweighted, never edited — paths whose five-year GDP growth sits near CBO potential and whose long-run rates sit near published medians count for more, outliers for less. Year-5 GDP growth: 2.3%.
Analyst-anchored
How it gets there: the first two years of every path are forced through the analyst baseline exactly; beyond that the model takes over, reweighted toward the analyst’s annual views. Year-5 GDP growth: 2.0%.
One worked example — the same path, judged four ways
Take the term spread: the 10-year yield minus the fed funds rate, which has averaged about 1.3pp since 1986. Suppose one simulated future lets the 10-year yield climb to 6% while fed funds settles at 3%, a 3pp gap that keeps widening. Model baseline has no opinion: nothing in its prior connects where the two rates end up. Theory-anchored objects at estimation time. Its prior treats a permanently widening spread as unlikely before any forecast is drawn, so coefficients that generate such paths carry less weight. Note what it does not do: it never says where either rate should be, only that the gap between them should not trend. Benchmark-anchored would not notice the spread at all, but would down-weight the same path afterwards if its five-year GDP growth strayed from 1.9%. Analyst-anchored would have pinned both rates directly for the first two years, leaving the far end to the model plus its annual tilts. Same model, same data: the four forecasts are these four judgements applied to every simulated path.
Source: FRED. Joshua Bailey Bayesian VAR · vintage 2026-07
